metadata.title: Patient-Reported Outcome Measures: Use in Medical Product Development to Support Labeling Claims — Guidance for Industry
metadata.document_version: Final Guidance — Clinical/Medical; issued by CDER, CBER, CDRH
metadata.extraction_note: IMPORTANT: The filename '33-physician-labeling-rule.pdf' is a mislabeled file. The actual document is the FDA December 2009 Guidance on Patient-Reported Outcome (PRO) Measures: Use in Medical Product Development to Support Labeling Claims. It is NOT the Physician Labeling Rule (PLR). The PLR (21 CFR 201.56/201.57) governs format/content of prescription drug labeling; this document governs PRO instrument development and validation for claims support. The v4 fda-labeling-be-rules.json covers PLR requirements; this document covers a different topic (PRO instrument methodology) and supplements v4 with clinical trial and instrument development requirements. Rule IDs use topic abbreviation PRO. Cross-references to 21 CFR 201.56 and 201.57 appear in this document's footnotes as the regulatory basis for labeling, but the substantive requirements here concern PRO instrument development.

rules.rule_text: A PRO instrument, including the questionnaire and all supporting information and documentation, must be shown to measure the treatment benefit or risk it claims to measure before it can support a labeling claim.
rules.source_section: Section I — Introduction
rules.verbatim_quote: A PRO instrument (i.e., a questionnaire plus the information and documentation that support its use) is a means to capture PRO data used to measure treatment benefit or risk in medical product clinical trials.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must provide documented evidence of patient input during PRO instrument development and of the instrument's performance in the specific application (population, condition) in which it is used.
rules.source_section: Section III — Evaluation of a PRO Instrument
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors should provide documented evidence of patient input during instrument development and of the instrument's performance in the specific application in which it is used (i.e., population, condition). An existing instrument can support a labeling claim if it can be shown to reliably measure the claimed concept in the patient population enrolled in the clinical trial.
rules.exceptions: An existing instrument may be used if it can be shown to reliably measure the claimed concept in the enrolled population.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must define the role a PRO endpoint will play in the clinical trial (primary, key secondary, or exploratory) so that appropriate statistical methods can be planned and applied. These approaches must be planned in an endpoint model.
rules.source_section: Section III.A — Endpoint Model
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors should define the role a PRO endpoint is intended to play in the clinical trial (i.e., a primary, key secondary, or exploratory endpoint) so that the instrument development and performance can be reviewed in the context of the intended role, and appropriate statistical methods can be planned and applied. It is critical to plan these approaches in what can be called an endpoint model.

rules.rule_text: An instrument's measurement properties must be well established before enrollment begins for confirmatory clinical trials. Sponsors must begin instrument development and evaluation early in medical product development and engage the FDA before confirmatory clinical trial protocols are finalized.
rules.source_section: Section III.B — Choice of PRO Instrument
rules.verbatim_quote: We suggest that an instrument's measurement properties be well established before enrollment begins for confirmatory clinical trials. Therefore, sponsors should begin instrument development and evaluation early in medical product development, and engage the FDA in a discussion about a new or unique PRO instrument before confirmatory clinical trial protocols are finalized.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must provide the FDA with documentation of PRO instrument development and testing in conjunction with clinical trial results to determine whether a labeling claim is substantiated. This documentation must address the conceptual framework, content validity, and other measurement properties.
rules.source_section: Section III.B — Choice of PRO Instrument
rules.verbatim_quote: The FDA will review documentation of PRO instrument development and testing in conjunction with clinical trial results to determine whether a labeling claim is substantiated.

rules.rule_text: The conceptual framework of a PRO instrument must explicitly define the concepts measured by the instrument in a diagram presenting the relationships between items, domains, and concepts measured and the scores produced.
rules.source_section: Section III.C — Conceptual Framework of a PRO Instrument
rules.verbatim_quote: The adequacy of a proposed instrument to support a claim depends on the conceptual framework of the PRO instrument. The conceptual framework explicitly defines the concepts measured by the instrument in a diagram that presents a description of the relationships between items, domain (subconcepts), and concepts measured and the scores produced by a PRO instrument.

rules.rule_text: Documentation of the instrument development process must reveal the means by which items and domains were identified. Exact words used to represent concepts measured must be derived using patient input to ensure conclusions drawn using instrument scores are valid.
rules.source_section: Section III.C.1 — Concepts Measured
rules.verbatim_quote: Documentation of the instrument development process should reveal the means by which the items and domains were identified. The exact words used to represent the concepts measured by domain or total scores should be derived using patient input to ensure the conclusions drawn using instrument scores are valid.

rules.rule_text: Content validity is the primary validity requirement for a PRO instrument; evidence of other types of validity or reliability will not overcome problems with content validity. Content validity must be established before other measurement properties are evaluated.
rules.source_section: Section III.D — Content Validity
rules.verbatim_quote: Evidence of other types of validity (e.g., construct validity) or reliability (e.g., consistent scores) will not overcome problems with content validity because we evaluate instrument adequacy to measure the concept represented by the labeling claim. It is important to establish content validity before other measurement properties are evaluated.

rules.rule_text: Item generation for PRO instruments must include input from the target patient population to establish items that reflect the concept of interest, covering variations in severity and population characteristics (age, sex, ethnicity, language) in accordance with the anticipated clinical trial design.
rules.source_section: Section III.D.1 — Item Generation
rules.verbatim_quote: Item generation should include input from the target patient population to establish the items that reflect the concept of interest and contribute to its evaluation... Item generation generally incorporates the input of a wide range of patients with the condition of interest to represent variations in severity and in population characteristics such as age, sex, ethnicity, and language groups in accordance with the anticipated clinical trial design.

rules.rule_text: Without adequate documentation of patient input, a PRO instrument's content validity is likely to be questioned. FDA will review documentation to determine that items cover all aspects of the concept important to patients and that saturation has been reached.
rules.source_section: Section III.D.1 — Item Generation
rules.verbatim_quote: Without adequate documentation of patient input, a PRO instrument's content validity is likely to be questioned. We will review documentation to determine that the items cover all aspects of the concept important to patients, and that saturation has been reached.

rules.rule_text: Items that ask patients to respond hypothetically are not recommended for clinical trials because they may cause patients to respond based on their desired condition rather than their actual condition.
rules.source_section: Section III.D.1 — Item Generation
rules.verbatim_quote: Items that ask patients to respond hypothetically may cause patients to respond on the basis of their desired condition rather than on their actual condition and therefore are not recommended for clinical trials.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must evaluate the rationale and appropriateness of the recall period for a PRO instrument, considering patient ability to validly recall the information requested. Items with short recall periods or that ask patients to describe their current or recent state are usually preferable.
rules.source_section: Section III.D.3 — Recall Period
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors should also evaluate the rationale and the appropriateness of the recall period for a PRO instrument. To this end, it is important to consider patient ability to validly recall the information requested... items with short recall periods or items that ask patients to describe their current or recent state are usually preferable.
rules.exceptions: If detailed recall of experience over a period of time is necessary, appropriate methods for enhancing validity and reliability of retrospectively reported data should be used (e.g., diary).

rules.rule_text: The PRO instrument format used in the clinical trial must be consistent with the format used during the instrument development process. FDA will review the specific format used in the clinical trial including the order and numbering of items, presentation of response options, grouping of items, and all instructions.
rules.source_section: Section III.D.5 — Instrument Format, Instructions, and Training
rules.verbatim_quote: It is important that the PRO instrument format used in the clinical trial be consistent with the format that is used during the instrument development process. Format refers to the exact questionnaire, diary, or interview script appearance used to collect the PRO data... We plan to review the specific format used in the clinical trial including the order and numbering of items, the presentation of response options in single response or grid formats, the grouping of items, patterns for skipping questions, and all instructions to interviewers or patients.

rules.rule_text: When developing or evaluating PRO instruments, sponsors must document pilot testing of whether patients understand items and instructions through cognitive interviewing, including confirmation that response options and recall period are appropriately comprehended.
rules.source_section: Section III.D.6 — Patient Understanding
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors are encouraged to examine all items and procedures in a pilot test of whether patients understand the items and instructions included in the PRO instrument. This examination should include documentation that the concepts represented in the PRO instrument's conceptual framework are confirmed, that the response options and recall period are appropriately comprehended, and that the instrument's readability is adequate for the intended population.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must justify the method chosen to combine items to create a score or to combine domain scores to create a general score, using qualitative research or defined statistical techniques.
rules.source_section: Section III.D.7 — Scoring of Items and Domains
rules.verbatim_quote: Using qualitative research or defined statistical techniques, sponsors should justify the method chosen to combine items to create a score or to combine domain scores to create a general score.
rules.exceptions: Use of empirically determined patient preference ratings to weight items or domains is discouraged unless the preference weights' relationship to the intended clinical trial population is known and found adequate and appropriate.

rules.rule_text: FDA will consider reliability, construct validity, and ability to detect change as additional measurement properties during review of a PRO instrument, after content validity has been established. These must be evaluated in the context of the specific clinical trial objectives, design, enrolled population, and SAP.
rules.source_section: Section III.E — Reliability, Other Validity, and Ability to Detect Change
rules.verbatim_quote: Once the instrument's content validity has been established, we intend to consider the following additional measurement properties during FDA review of a PRO instrument: reliability, construct validity, and ability to detect change. We plan to review the measurement properties that are specific to the documented PRO instrument's conceptual framework, confirmed scoring algorithm, administration procedures, and questionnaire format in light of the clinical trial's objectives, design, enrolled population, and statistical analysis plan (SAP).

rules.rule_text: FDA will review documentation of test-retest reliability. Test-retest is most informative when the time interval is long enough in stable patients to minimize memory effects. Internal consistency reliability alone may not constitute sufficient evidence of reliability for clinical trial purposes.
rules.source_section: Section III.E.1 — Reliability
rules.verbatim_quote: We will review documentation of tests to determine if reproducibility (e.g., test-retest reliability) has been demonstrated. Test-retest is most informative when the time interval chosen between the test and retest is long enough in stable patients to minimize memory effects... Internal consistency reliability tests (e.g., Cronbach's alpha) to determine agreement among responses to different questions, in the absence of test-retest reliability, may not constitute sufficient evidence of reliability for clinical trial purposes.
rules.exceptions: For remitting and relapsing or episodic diseases, test-retest reliability may be difficult or impossible to establish.

rules.rule_text: When a PRO instrument is modified, sponsors must provide evidence to confirm the new instrument's adequacy, including its content validity in the new population or application context.
rules.source_section: Section III.F — Instrument Modification
rules.verbatim_quote: When a PRO instrument is modified, sponsors generally should provide evidence to confirm the new instrument's adequacy... If the PRO instrument will be used in a significantly different patient population (e.g., a different disease or age group), we may recommend using qualitative studies to confirm content validity in the new population.
rules.exceptions: Not every small change in application or format necessitates extensive studies to document the final version's measurement properties.

rules.rule_text: For pediatric and cognitively impaired populations, FDA discourages proxy-reported outcome measures. For patients who cannot respond for themselves, FDA encourages observer reports that include only those events or behaviors that can be observed.
rules.source_section: Section III.G.1 — Children and Adolescents; Section III.G.2 — Patients Cognitively Impaired or Unable to Communicate
rules.verbatim_quote: We discourage proxy-reported outcome measures for this population (children and adolescents)... For patients who cannot respond for themselves (e.g., infant patients), we encourage observer reports that include only those events or behaviors that can be observed... We discourage proxy-reported outcome measures for this population (cognitively impaired). For patients who cannot respond for themselves (e.g., cognitively impaired), we encourage observer reports that include only those events or behaviors that can be observed.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must provide evidence that the content validity and other measurement properties of PRO instrument versions used in multinational trials are adequately similar between all language and culture versions used in the clinical trial.
rules.source_section: Section III.G.3 — Culture or Language Subgroups
rules.verbatim_quote: We recommend that sponsors provide evidence that the content validity and other measurement properties are adequately similar between all versions used in the clinical trial. We will review the process used to translate and culturally adapt the instrument for populations that will use them in the trial.

rules.rule_text: If the PRO measurement goal is to support labeling claims, PRO concept measurement must be stated as a specific clinical trial objective or hypothesis. The case report form in the protocol must include the exact format and version of the specific PRO instrument to be administered.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A — General Protocol Considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: If the PRO measurement goal is to support labeling claims, PRO concept measurement should be stated as a specific clinical trial objective or hypothesis. It is important that the case report form in the protocol include the exact format and version of the specific PRO instrument to be administered.

rules.rule_text: Open-label clinical trials are rarely adequate to support labeling claims based on PRO instruments. PRO instruments administered during a clinic visit must be administered before other clinical assessments or procedures to prevent influencing patient perceptions.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A.1 — Blinding and Randomization
rules.verbatim_quote: Open-label clinical trials, where patients and investigators are aware of assigned therapy, are rarely adequate to support labeling claims based on PRO instruments... to prevent influencing patient perceptions, PRO instruments administered during a clinic visit should be administered before other clinical assessments or procedures.
rules.exceptions: There are certain situations, such as evaluation of some medical devices or administration of identifiable treatment regimens, where blinding is not feasible.

rules.rule_text: The clinical trial protocol must describe how missing PRO data will be handled in the analysis. Patients should remain in the clinical trial even if they have discontinued treatment and should continue to provide PRO data.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A.3 — Handling Missing Data
rules.verbatim_quote: The clinical trial protocol should describe how missing data will be handled in the analysis... Patients should remain in the clinical trial, even if they have discontinued treatment, and should continue to provide PRO data.

rules.rule_text: The clinical trial protocol must define the endpoint measures and the criteria for statistical analysis and interpretation of results, including a specification of the conditions for a positive clinical trial conclusion, before data are unblinded.
rules.source_section: Section IV.D — Design Considerations for Multiple Endpoints
rules.verbatim_quote: It is critical that the clinical trial protocol define the endpoint measures and the criteria for the statistical analysis and interpretation of results, including a specification of the conditions for a positive clinical trial conclusion, because determination of these criteria and conditions after data are unblinded will not be credible.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must avoid cherry picking or post hoc selective picking of PRO endpoint results for inclusion in proposed labeling. Sponsors must also avoid separate consideration of PRO endpoints from the clinical trial's primary objectives in terms of clinical trial design or data analysis.
rules.source_section: Section IV.D — Design Considerations for Multiple Endpoints
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors should avoid separate consideration of PRO endpoints from the clinical trial's primary objectives in terms of clinical trial design or data analysis. Sponsors also should avoid cherry picking or post hoc selective picking of PRO endpoint results for inclusion in proposed labeling.

rules.rule_text: When PRO instruments are used, sponsors must ensure that FDA regulatory requirements are met for sponsor and investigator record keeping, maintenance, and access. For electronic PRO instruments, the clinical trial protocol or a separate document must specify how the electronic PRO source data will be maintained and how the investigator will meet regulatory requirements.
rules.source_section: Section IV.F — Specific Concerns When Using Electronic PRO Instruments
rules.verbatim_quote: When PRO instruments are used, sponsors must ensure that FDA regulatory requirements are met for sponsor and investigator record keeping, maintenance, and access... The clinical trial protocol, or a separate document, should specify how the electronic PRO source data will be maintained and how the investigator will meet the regulatory requirements.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must avoid direct PRO data transmission from the PRO data collection device to the sponsor, clinical investigator, or other third party without an electronic audit trail that documents all changes to the data after it leaves the PRO data collection device.
rules.source_section: Section IV.F — Specific Concerns When Using Electronic PRO Instruments
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors also should avoid the following: Direct PRO data transmission from the PRO data collection device to the sponsor, clinical investigator, or other third party without an electronic audit trail that documents all changes to the data after it leaves the PRO data collection device.

rules.rule_text: Electronic PRO data must be consistent with computerized systems data standards per 21 CFR part 11 guidance. Sponsors must establish appropriate system and security controls, and cyber-security and system maintenance plans that address how to ensure data integrity during network attacks and software updates.
rules.source_section: Section IV.F — Specific Concerns When Using Electronic PRO Instruments
rules.verbatim_quote: Because electronic PRO data (including data gathered by personal digital assistants or phone-based interactive voice recording systems) are part of the case history, electronic PRO data should be consistent with the data standards described in that guidance. Sponsors should plan to establish appropriate system and security controls, as well as cyber-security and system maintenance plans that address how to ensure data integrity during network attacks and software updates.

rules.rule_text: Every clinical trial protocol must describe principal data analysis features in the statistical section with a detailed elaboration in a SAP. Unplanned or post hoc statistical analyses conducted after unblinding are viewed as exploratory and cannot serve as the basis of a labeling claim of effectiveness.
rules.source_section: Section V.A — General Statistical Considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: Every protocol should describe the principal data analysis features in the statistical section with a detailed elaboration of the analysis in an SAP... We usually view unplanned or post hoc statistical analyses conducted after unblinding as exploratory and, therefore, unable to serve as the basis of a labeling claim of effectiveness.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must provide the FDA with the clinical trial's SAP for review. The SAP must describe the planned primary analysis in detail noting whether the endpoint will be analyzed as a continuous variable, dichotomous variable, or graded response; adjustments for multiplicity; and the specific statistical methods planned.
rules.source_section: Section V.B — Statistical Considerations for Using Multiple Endpoints
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors should provide the FDA with the clinical trial's SAP for review... The SAP should describe the planned primary analysis in detail noting whether the endpoint will be analyzed as a continuous variable (mean scores), dichotomous variable (success or failure), or some graded response; the primary and secondary endpoints; adjustments for multiplicity to control the overall Type I error rate; and the specific statistical methods planned.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must avoid proposing labeling claims based on statistical significance alone, because statistical significance can sometimes be achieved for small changes in PRO measures that may not be clinically meaningful.
rules.source_section: Section V.E — Interpretation of Clinical Trial Results
rules.verbatim_quote: Because statistical significance can sometimes be achieved for small changes in PRO measures that may not be clinically meaningful (i.e., do not indicate treatment benefit), we encourage sponsors to avoid proposing labeling claims based on statistical significance alone.

rules.rule_text: Rules for handling missing PRO data must be specific to each PRO instrument and must usually be determined during the instrument development process. The SAP must specify all rules for handling missing data.
rules.source_section: Section V.D.1 — Missing Items within Domains
rules.verbatim_quote: Rules for handling missing data should be specific to each PRO instrument and usually should be determined during the instrument development process. The SAP should specify all rules for handling missing data. For example, the SAP can specify the proportion of items that can be missing before a domain is treated as missing.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must propose two or more sensitivity analyses with different methods for missing data imputation in the clinical trial protocol to assess the robustness of statistical estimation for endpoints with missing data.
rules.source_section: Section V.D.2 — Missing Entire Domains or Entire Measurements
rules.verbatim_quote: We recommend that in the protocol the sponsor propose two or more sensitivity analyses with different methods for missing data imputation.

rules.rule_text: For confirming a complex, multidomain labeling claim, a single-item PRO instrument or instruments measuring only a simple concept are not adequate. PRO-based evidence of improved symptoms alone will only support claims specific to improvement of those symptoms.
rules.source_section: Section III.B — Choice of PRO Instrument
rules.verbatim_quote: If the measurement goal is to support a complex, multidomain concept, PRO instruments that measure a simple concept may not be adequate to substantiate the complex claim. For example, PRO-based evidence of improved symptoms alone will only support claims specific to improvement of the symptoms and would not support a general claim related to improvement in a patient's ability to function or the patient's psychological state.

rules.rule_text: For PRO instruments used across multiple cultures or languages, sponsors must provide evidence that content validity and other measurement properties are adequately similar between all versions. FDA will review the process used to translate and culturally adapt the instrument.
rules.source_section: Section III.G.3 — Culture or Language Subgroups
rules.verbatim_quote: Regardless of whether the instrument was developed concurrently in multiple cultures or languages or whether a fully developed instrument was adapted or translated to new cultures or languages, we recommend that sponsors provide evidence that the content validity and other measurement properties are adequately similar between all versions used in the clinical trial. We will review the process used to translate and culturally adapt the instrument for populations that will use them in the trial.
